questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
The Second Life of Glass
For years, Clearview County sent most broken glass from recycling bins to a landfill. The problem was not that glass could not be recycled. It was that jars and bottles often broke during collection, mixed with food scraps and paper, and became too contaminated for traditional glass manufacturers to use. Hauling the broken material to a distant processing plant also cost more than the county could afford.
Last year, the public works department tested a different approach. Instead of trying to turn broken glass back into bottles, workers crushed cleaned glass into a sand-like material called glass aggregate. The county then used the aggregate in a drainage layer under a new walking path at Riverside Park. Engineers tested the material to make sure sharp edges were removed and water could pass through properly.
The pilot project did not solve every recycling problem. Residents still needed to rinse containers, and glass mixed with trash could not be used. The county also had to buy equipment to clean and crush the material. However, the project reduced landfill waste and replaced some purchased gravel.
Public works director Maribel Stone said the project changed how her department viewed glass. “We stopped asking whether every bottle could become another bottle,” she explained. “We started asking whether the material could still serve a useful purpose.” If the next two projects perform well, the county may use glass aggregate under sidewalks, parking areas, and drainage trenches.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Clearview County found a practical way to reuse some broken glass even when it could not be recycled into new bottles.
choices.B: Clearview County stopped accepting glass from residents because recycling is impossible.
choices.C: Riverside Park was closed because glass aggregate made the walking path unsafe.
choices.D: Traditional glass manufacturers asked the county to stop cleaning bottles and jars.

questions.correctAnswerText: Clearview County found a practical way to reuse some broken glass even when it could not be recycled into new bottles.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that the county reused broken glass as construction aggregate rather than sending it to a landfill.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Second Life of Glass.”
Why had Clearview County sent much of its broken glass to a landfill?

choices.A: The county had no residents who used glass containers.
choices.B: Broken glass often became contaminated and was costly to haul for traditional recycling.
choices.C: Engineers refused to test recycled materials.
choices.D: Glass aggregate could not be used in any construction project.

questions.correctAnswerText: Broken glass often became contaminated and was costly to haul for traditional recycling.
questions.explanation: The passage says contamination and hauling costs made traditional recycling difficult.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Second Life of Glass.”
Which TWO steps were part of the county’s pilot project?

choices.A: Cleaning and crushing glass into a sand-like material
choices.B: Using glass aggregate under a walking path
choices.C: Sending all glass to a distant bottle factory
choices.D: Mixing glass with food scraps before use
choices.E: Closing all recycling centers in the county

questions.correctAnswerText: Cleaning and crushing glass into a sand-like material | Using glass aggregate under a walking path
questions.explanation: The pilot involved cleaning and crushing glass and using it under a Riverside Park walking path.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Second Life of Glass.”
What can be inferred from Maribel Stone’s statement?

choices.A: The county began thinking more flexibly about how discarded materials could be reused.
choices.B: The county decided that glass had no value after it was broken.
choices.C: The county believed every bottle must become another bottle to be useful.
choices.D: The county planned to remove the walking path from Riverside Park.

questions.correctAnswerText: The county began thinking more flexibly about how discarded materials could be reused.
questions.explanation: Stone’s statement shows that the county considered new uses for glass instead of only bottle-to-bottle recycling.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Second Life of Glass.”
In the sentence “The pilot project did not solve every recycling problem,” the word pilot most nearly means [D1].
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=trial or test
questions.explanation: In context, pilot means a small trial project used to test an idea.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Second Life of Glass.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Engineers tested the material to make sure sharp edges were removed.
tiles.B: The county reduced landfill waste and replaced some purchased gravel.
tiles.C: Glass mixed with trash could not be used.
tiles.D: The county turned every bottle into a new bottle.

targets.T3: Evidence that the project had limits

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Engineers tested the material to make sure sharp edges were removed.; T2=The county reduced landfill waste and replaced some purchased gravel.; T3=Glass mixed with trash could not be used.
questions.explanation: Each tile provides the requested evidence about safety, benefit, and limitation.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Meal Break Coverage Procedure
To: Front Desk and Fitness Floor Staff
From: Lakeside Recreation Center Management
Subject: Meal Break Coverage Procedure
Beginning Monday, all staff members must record meal break coverage on the shared schedule before leaving their assigned area. This procedure applies to the front desk, fitness floor, child activity room, and pool entrance.
Before a meal break begins, the employee leaving the area must confirm that another trained staff member is present and has accepted coverage. Coverage may not be provided by an employee who has not completed the required training for that area. For example, a fitness floor employee may not cover the pool entrance unless that employee has completed pool check-in procedures and emergency communication training.
The employee taking the break should write the start time, expected return time, and covering employee’s initials on the shared schedule. If the covering employee is called away for an emergency, the manager on duty must be notified immediately.
This procedure is not meant to shorten breaks. It is meant to prevent unattended service areas and reduce confusion when members need help. Staff who have questions about coverage eligibility should speak with a shift lead before their scheduled break time.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To explain how staff should arrange and record coverage before meal breaks
choices.B: To cancel meal breaks for front desk and fitness floor employees
choices.C: To allow any employee to cover any work area without training
choices.D: To announce that the recreation center will close during lunch hours

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain how staff should arrange and record coverage before meal breaks
questions.explanation: The document gives the procedure for confirming and recording coverage before staff leave for meal breaks.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Meal Break Coverage Procedure.”
Why may a fitness floor employee be unable to cover the pool entrance?

choices.A: The employee may not have completed pool check-in and emergency communication training.
choices.B: The employee may prefer to work only at the front desk.
choices.C: The pool entrance closes before all meal breaks.
choices.D: The shared schedule cannot be used for pool staff.

questions.correctAnswerText: The employee may not have completed pool check-in and emergency communication training.
questions.explanation: The document says staff may cover only areas for which they have completed required training.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Meal Break Coverage Procedure.”
Which TWO details must be written on the shared schedule?

choices.A: The start time of the break
choices.B: The expected return time
choices.C: The employee’s favorite work area
choices.D: The number of members in the building
choices.E: The price of a recreation center membership

questions.correctAnswerText: The start time of the break | The expected return time
questions.explanation: The procedure requires the start time, expected return time, and covering employee’s initials.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Meal Break Coverage Procedure.”
What should happen if the covering employee is called away for an emergency?

choices.A: The manager on duty should be notified immediately.
choices.B: The break should automatically become unpaid.
choices.C: Members should be told to leave the building.
choices.D: The shared schedule should be thrown away.

questions.correctAnswerText: The manager on duty should be notified immediately.
questions.explanation: The document states that the manager must be notified if the covering employee is called away.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Meal Break Coverage Procedure.”
Which sentence best explains the reason for the new procedure?

choices.A: “Beginning Monday, all staff members must record meal break coverage on the shared schedule.”
choices.B: “This procedure applies to the front desk, fitness floor, child activity room, and pool entrance.”
choices.C: “It is meant to prevent unattended service areas and reduce confusion when members need help.”
choices.D: “Staff who have questions about coverage eligibility should speak with a shift lead.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “It is meant to prevent unattended service areas and reduce confusion when members need help.”
questions.explanation: This sentence explains the reason for the procedure.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Why Neighborhood Tool Training Matters
The city should fund short tool-safety workshops at community centers. Many residents want to complete simple home projects, such as hanging shelves, repairing a loose step, or installing weather stripping, but they may not know how to use basic tools safely. A free ninety-minute workshop can prevent injuries and make small repairs less intimidating.
The need is practical. Staff at the North Park Community Center surveyed adults who attended a housing fair and found that many owned a drill or saw but had never received instruction on safe use. Several said they delayed minor repairs because they were afraid of damaging a wall or hurting themselves. When small problems are delayed, they can become larger and more expensive.
Tool-safety workshops could also support renters. A workshop should not encourage renters to make repairs that violate a lease. Instead, it can teach residents how to identify simple tasks they may handle, when to ask a landlord, and how to describe a problem clearly when requesting maintenance.
Some people argue that tool use is a private matter and not a city responsibility. However, community centers already teach practical skills such as computer use, first aid, and financial basics. Safe tool use fits the same purpose: helping residents manage ordinary problems with more confidence. The city does not need a large program. A few workshops each season would be a reasonable start.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: The city should fund short tool-safety workshops because they can prevent injuries and help residents handle simple problems.
choices.B: The city should require every resident to buy power tools.
choices.C: Renters should repair all building problems without contacting landlords.
choices.D: Community centers should stop offering practical classes.

questions.correctAnswerText: The city should fund short tool-safety workshops because they can prevent injuries and help residents handle simple problems.
questions.explanation: The author argues that tool-safety workshops offer practical benefits for residents.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Why Neighborhood Tool Training Matters.”
Which evidence best supports the claim that residents need tool-safety instruction?

choices.A: Community centers already teach computer use.
choices.B: The city does not need a large program.
choices.C: Many adults surveyed owned tools but had never received instruction on safe use.
choices.D: Some people believe tool use is a private matter.

questions.correctAnswerText: Many adults surveyed owned tools but had never received instruction on safe use.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports the need for instruction.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Why Neighborhood Tool Training Matters.”
Which TWO benefits of tool-safety workshops does the author describe?

choices.A: They can help residents avoid injuries.
choices.B: They can help residents understand when to ask a landlord.
choices.C: They guarantee that all home repairs will be free.
choices.D: They allow renters to ignore lease rules.
choices.E: They replace the need for professional contractors in every situation.

questions.correctAnswerText: They can help residents avoid injuries. | They can help residents understand when to ask a landlord.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that workshops can improve safety and help renters understand appropriate action.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Why Neighborhood Tool Training Matters.”
How does the author respond to the concern that tool use is not a city responsibility?

choices.A: By arguing that community centers already teach other practical life skills
choices.B: By saying that residents should never use tools at home
choices.C: By claiming that landlords should attend every workshop
choices.D: By suggesting that tool workshops should replace first aid classes

questions.correctAnswerText: By arguing that community centers already teach other practical life skills
questions.explanation: The author responds by comparing tool safety to other practical skills taught at community centers.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Why Neighborhood Tool Training Matters.”
Choose the sentence that would best strengthen the author’s argument if added after paragraph 2: [D1]

D1: A local clinic reported treating several minor hand injuries last year from improper use of household tools.
The workshop room at North Park has twelve folding chairs.
Some residents prefer weekend classes to weekday classes.

questions.correct: D1=A local clinic reported treating several minor hand injuries last year from improper use of household tools.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=A local clinic reported treating several minor hand injuries last year from improper use of household tools.
questions.explanation: This sentence strengthens the argument by giving evidence of a local safety problem.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Why Neighborhood Tool Training Matters.”
Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: Brief instruction can help people use common tools more safely and confidently.
choices.B: Every resident wants to become a licensed carpenter.
choices.C: Community centers should teach only classes that use computers.
choices.D: No home repair task ever requires professional help.

questions.correctAnswerText: Brief instruction can help people use common tools more safely and confidently.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the idea that short workshops can make a meaningful difference.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The Last Chair
When the auditorium emptied after graduation, Mr. Alvarez stayed behind to stack chairs. He had been the school custodian for twenty-two years, long enough to recognize families by the way they searched for seats. Tonight he had watched parents cheer, younger siblings wave programs, and graduates cross the stage with careful steps, as if each footfall carried more than a diploma.
He was almost finished when he noticed one chair still facing the stage. A blue program lay on the seat. On the back page, someone had written, “For Mom, who worked nights so I could finish.” Mr. Alvarez picked it up gently.
He could have placed it in the trash with the empty water bottles and curled ribbons. Instead, he walked to the office and set it on the counter where graduates returned for records and transcripts. He added a note: “Found after ceremony.”
The next morning, a young woman came in before the office officially opened. She wore jeans instead of a cap and gown, but Mr. Alvarez remembered her. She had paused at the top of the stage steps the night before, looking into the crowd before taking her diploma.
“My program,” she said when she saw it. “I thought I lost it.”
Mr. Alvarez nodded. “Important things have a way of waiting.”
After she left, he returned to the auditorium. The chairs were stacked, the stage was bare, and the room looked ordinary again. But for a moment, he could still hear the applause.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Mr. Alvarez must decide what to do with a program that has personal meaning.
choices.B: Mr. Alvarez argues with graduates about where to sit.
choices.C: A graduate refuses to return to the school office.
choices.D: The auditorium cannot be cleaned after graduation.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mr. Alvarez must decide what to do with a program that has personal meaning.
questions.explanation: The story centers on Mr. Alvarez finding the program and choosing to preserve it for its owner.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Last Chair.”
Why does Mr. Alvarez avoid throwing away the program?

choices.A: He recognizes that it may have emotional value to its owner.
choices.B: He wants to use it as a cleaning checklist.
choices.C: He is required to keep every program for twenty-two years.
choices.D: He plans to display it permanently on the stage.

questions.correctAnswerText: He recognizes that it may have emotional value to its owner.
questions.explanation: The note on the program suggests it is meaningful, so Mr. Alvarez treats it carefully.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Last Chair.”
What does the handwritten note on the program reveal?

choices.A: The graduate connects her accomplishment to her mother’s sacrifice.
choices.B: The graduate wants the auditorium chairs replaced.
choices.C: The program belongs to Mr. Alvarez.
choices.D: The ceremony was canceled before the diplomas were given.

questions.correctAnswerText: The graduate connects her accomplishment to her mother’s sacrifice.
questions.explanation: The note thanks the graduate’s mother for working nights so the graduate could finish.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Last Chair.”
Which TWO details show Mr. Alvarez’s thoughtfulness?

choices.A: He picks up the program gently.
choices.B: He places the program in the office with a note.
choices.C: He throws away the program with empty water bottles.
choices.D: He leaves the auditorium before stacking the chairs.
choices.E: He refuses to speak to the young woman.

questions.correctAnswerText: He picks up the program gently. | He places the program in the office with a note.
questions.explanation: These actions show that Mr. Alvarez handles the program thoughtfully.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Last Chair.”
What does Mr. Alvarez mean when he says, “Important things have a way of waiting”?

choices.A: Some meaningful items deserve care and may find their way back to the right person.
choices.B: Graduation ceremonies should always begin late.
choices.C: Important objects should be locked away forever.
choices.D: Lost programs are never returned to students.

questions.correctAnswerText: Some meaningful items deserve care and may find their way back to the right person.
questions.explanation: Mr. Alvarez’s comment reflects his belief that the program’s importance deserved patience and care.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Last Chair.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Mr. Alvarez finds a program with a personal note.
tiles.B: He sets the program on the office counter with a “Found after ceremony” note.
tiles.C: The young woman returns and recognizes the program as hers.
tiles.D: Mr. Alvarez cancels the graduation ceremony.

targets.T2: The action that protects the item

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Mr. Alvarez finds a program with a personal note.; T2=He sets the program on the office counter with a “Found after ceremony” note.; T3=The young woman returns and recognizes the program as hers.
questions.explanation: The tiles identify the discovery, Mr. Alvarez’s protective action, and the resolution.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
How Public Comments Work
City councils often invite public comments before voting on local issues. A public comment period gives residents a chance to share concerns, support, questions, or personal experiences related to a decision. These comments can help elected officials understand how a proposal may affect people in different parts of the community.
Public comments are usually limited by time. A speaker may have two or three minutes, depending on the meeting rules. This limit can feel strict, but it allows more people to participate. Residents who want to speak effectively should prepare one main point, give a specific example, and end with a clear request. Reading a long history of the issue is usually less useful than explaining how the proposal affects the speaker or the community.
Written comments can also be important. Some residents cannot attend evening meetings because of work, childcare, transportation, or health concerns. Many councils accept emails or online forms before the meeting. Written comments may be included in the meeting record and reviewed by officials before a vote.
Public comments do not guarantee that officials will agree with every speaker. They are one part of the decision-making process, along with laws, budgets, staff reports, and competing community needs. Still, comments can shape the discussion by adding information that may not appear in official reports.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Public comments give residents a way to share views with officials, but they are only one part of local decision-making.
choices.B: Public comments guarantee that city councils will approve every resident’s request.
choices.C: Residents should avoid preparing before speaking at public meetings.
choices.D: Written comments are never reviewed by elected officials.

questions.correctAnswerText: Public comments give residents a way to share views with officials, but they are only one part of local decision-making.
questions.explanation: The passage explains both the purpose and the limits of public comments.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “How Public Comments Work.”
Why are public comments usually limited by time?

choices.A: To allow more people to participate
choices.B: To prevent written comments from being accepted
choices.C: To make sure only city employees can speak
choices.D: To avoid including comments in the meeting record

questions.correctAnswerText: To allow more people to participate
questions.explanation: Time limits help more speakers have a chance to comment.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “How Public Comments Work.”
Which advice does the passage give for speaking effectively?

choices.A: Prepare one main point and give a specific example.
choices.B: Read a long history of every city issue.
choices.C: Speak without mentioning how the proposal affects anyone.
choices.D: Use the entire meeting to discuss unrelated topics.

questions.correctAnswerText: Prepare one main point and give a specific example.
questions.explanation: The passage recommends preparing one main point, giving a specific example, and making a clear request.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “How Public Comments Work.”
What can be inferred about written comments?

choices.A: They can make participation possible for residents who cannot attend meetings.
choices.B: They are accepted only from residents who speak in person.
choices.C: They automatically decide the outcome of every vote.
choices.D: They are less important than comments made by city staff only.

questions.correctAnswerText: They can make participation possible for residents who cannot attend meetings.
questions.explanation: Written comments help residents participate despite barriers such as work, childcare, transportation, or health concerns.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “How Public Comments Work.”
How is the passage mainly organized?

choices.A: It explains the purpose of public comments, gives practical advice, and describes their limits.
choices.B: It tells a story about one mayor’s first election.
choices.C: It lists every law that controls city council meetings.
choices.D: It argues that residents should stop attending public meetings.

questions.correctAnswerText: It explains the purpose of public comments, gives practical advice, and describes their limits.
questions.explanation: The passage describes why comments exist, how to use them effectively, and why they do not guarantee agreement.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Volunteer Translation Desk
(1) The community health fair will include a volunteer translation desk near the entrance. (2) Visitors who need help understanding forms may ask for assistance in Spanish, Vietnamese, or Arabic. (3) Volunteers will not give medical advice, but they can help visitors read directions and locate the correct information table. (4) [D1], each volunteer will wear a badge showing the languages they speak. (5) The desk [D2] open from 9:00 a.m. to 3:00 p.m. (6) Organizers hope the desk will make the fair easier to use for more families.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=For this reason; D2=will be
questions.explanation: The badge information follows from the need to identify language abilities, and “will be open” is the correct verb phrase.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Volunteer Translation Desk.”
Which revision of sentence 6 is most concise while keeping the meaning?

choices.A: Organizers hope the desk will make the fair easier to use for more families.
choices.B: Organizers are hopeful that the desk will be something that makes the health fair easier for use by more families.
choices.C: Organizers hope, with hope, that more families will use the fair with ease because of the desk.
choices.D: The desk is hoped by organizers to make easier use of the fair for families.

questions.correctAnswerText: Organizers hope the desk will make the fair easier to use for more families.
questions.explanation: This choice is clear and concise.

choices.A: The badges for the volunteers is printed in large type.
choices.B: The list of available languages are posted near the entrance.

choices.D: The table with health forms have been moved.

questions.correctAnswerText: The volunteers who speak Arabic arrive at nine.
questions.explanation: The plural subject “volunteers” correctly takes the plural verb “arrive.”

questions.explanation: Repeating “Priya” makes clear who attached the slides.

questions.prompt:

The store expected the delivery to arrive before noon. [D1], the truck was delayed by highway construction.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=However
questions.explanation: “However” signals contrast between the expected delivery time and the delay.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?

choices.A: Because the printer ran out of ink the clerk opened a new cartridge.
choices.B: Because the printer ran out of ink, the clerk opened a new cartridge.
choices.C: Because, the printer ran out of ink the clerk opened a new cartridge.
choices.D: Because the printer, ran out of ink the clerk opened a new cartridge.

questions.correctAnswerText: Because the printer ran out of ink, the clerk opened a new cartridge.
questions.explanation: The introductory dependent clause should be followed by a comma.

questions.prompt:
Which revision best combines the sentences?
The shelter opened a resource room. The room provides job-search computers.

choices.A: The shelter opened a resource room that provides job-search computers.
choices.B: The room opened the shelter and provides job-search computers.
choices.C: Providing the shelter, the computers opened a resource room.
choices.D: The shelter opened, and the resource room computers provide it.

questions.prompt: Which sentence maintains consistent verb tense?

choices.A: The nurse checked the form and records the visit yesterday.
choices.B: The nurse checks the form and recorded the visit yesterday.
choices.C: The nurse checked the form and recorded the visit yesterday.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Keep Printed Bus Schedules
The transit agency should continue providing printed bus schedules at major stops, libraries, and senior centers. Digital schedules are useful, but not every rider has reliable phone service, a charged battery, or comfort using apps. A printed schedule can be read quickly without an account, data plan, or device.
Printed schedules are especially important when routes change. Riders may need to compare the new timetable with the old one, plan transfers, or share information with someone who does not use a smartphone. A paper schedule posted at a bus stop can also help occasional riders who are unfamiliar with the route.
The agency can reduce waste without eliminating printed schedules. It can print fewer copies, update them only when routes change, and use clear signs directing riders to online updates when possible. Public transportation works best when information is available in more than one format. Removing paper completely would make the system harder for some riders to use.
Passage B: Move Bus Information Mostly Online
The transit agency should move most bus schedule information online. Printed schedules become outdated quickly, especially when construction, weather, or staffing shortages require temporary changes. Riders who depend on paper may miss important updates. Online schedules can be revised immediately and can show real-time arrival information that paper cannot provide.
Printing and distributing schedules also costs money that could be used for service improvements. Many riders already check arrival times on phones before leaving home or work. The agency should invest in a simple, mobile-friendly website and electronic signs at the busiest stops.
Accessibility still matters. The agency should provide printed route summaries for riders who request them and post customer-service phone numbers at stops. However, maintaining large stacks of printed schedules at many locations is no longer the best use of resources. A mostly online system, supported by targeted paper options, would give riders more current information.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A supports keeping printed schedules widely available, while Passage B supports moving most schedule information online with limited paper support.
choices.B: Passage A argues that buses should stop running, while Passage B argues that all stops should be removed.
choices.C: Passage A supports only smartphone apps, while Passage B opposes all electronic signs.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on driver uniforms, while Passage B focuses on bus seat colors.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A supports keeping printed schedules widely available, while Passage B supports moving most schedule information online with limited paper support.
questions.explanation: The passages disagree about how much the transit agency should rely on printed schedules.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about bus schedules.
Which evidence most directly supports Passage A’s argument?

choices.A: Not every rider has reliable phone service, a charged battery, or comfort using apps.
choices.B: Online schedules can show real-time arrival information.
choices.C: Printing and distributing schedules costs money.
choices.D: Electronic signs could be placed at the busiest stops.

questions.correctAnswerText: Not every rider has reliable phone service, a charged battery, or comfort using apps.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports Passage A’s accessibility argument.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to the paired passages about bus schedules.
Which TWO claims from Passage B are supported with reasons or evidence?

choices.A: Online schedules can be updated quickly during temporary changes.
choices.B: Online schedules can show real-time arrival information.
choices.C: Printed schedules are always illegal at bus stops.
choices.D: Riders never need customer-service phone numbers.
choices.E: Paper schedules can show live bus locations more accurately than websites.

questions.correctAnswerText: Online schedules can be updated quickly during temporary changes. | Online schedules can show real-time arrival information.
questions.explanation: Passage B gives these as advantages of online schedule information.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about bus schedules.
Which statement from Passage B directly addresses the accessibility concern raised in Passage A?

choices.A: “The transit agency should move most bus schedule information online.”
choices.B: “Online schedules can be revised immediately.”
choices.C: “The agency should provide printed route summaries for riders who request them and post customer-service phone numbers at stops.”
choices.D: “Many riders already check arrival times on phones before leaving home or work.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “The agency should provide printed route summaries for riders who request them and post customer-service phone numbers at stops.”
questions.explanation: This sentence responds to concerns about riders who may not use digital schedules.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about bus schedules.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: The transit agency must balance current information, cost, and rider accessibility.
choices.B: All riders already use the same type of phone.
choices.C: Bus routes never change after schedules are printed.
choices.D: Printed schedules are the only possible source of transit information.

questions.correctAnswerText: The transit agency must balance current information, cost, and rider accessibility.
questions.explanation: Both passages discuss how to provide schedule information effectively while considering different rider needs.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about bus schedules.
Which additional evidence would most help evaluate both arguments?

choices.A: Data on how many riders use printed schedules, online schedules, phone support, and electronic signs
choices.B: A list of bus paint colors from the past ten years
choices.C: A history of the first bus route in the state
choices.D: A survey asking riders which bus seats are most comfortable

questions.correctAnswerText: Data on how many riders use printed schedules, online schedules, phone support, and electronic signs
questions.explanation: This data would help judge which information formats riders actually use.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Stored in a locked cabinet, Dana handed the visitor badges to the receptionist.

choices.A: Stored in a locked cabinet, Dana handed the visitor badges to the receptionist.
choices.B: Dana handed the visitor badges stored in a locked cabinet to the receptionist.
choices.C: Dana stored in a locked cabinet handed the visitor badges to the receptionist.
choices.D: The receptionist handed Dana stored visitor badges in a locked cabinet.

questions.correctAnswerText: Dana handed the visitor badges stored in a locked cabinet to the receptionist.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to “visitor badges,” the items stored in the cabinet.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The class watched a demonstration. The demonstration showed how to file taxes online.

choices.A: The class watched a demonstration showing how to file taxes online.
choices.B: The demonstration watched the class and filed taxes online.
choices.C: The class watched, and taxes showed how to file the demonstration online.
choices.D: Showing the class online, the taxes watched a demonstration.

questions.correctAnswerText: The class watched a demonstration showing how to file taxes online.
questions.explanation: This sentence clearly combines the two ideas.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses commas correctly?

choices.A: The orientation, which begins at nine, will include a campus tour.
choices.B: The orientation which begins, at nine will include a campus tour.
choices.C: The orientation, which begins at nine will include, a campus tour.
choices.D: The orientation which, begins at nine, will include a campus tour.

questions.correctAnswerText: The orientation, which begins at nine, will include a campus tour.
questions.explanation: The nonessential clause “which begins at nine” is correctly set off by commas.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Require Basic First Aid Training for High School Students
High schools should require students to complete basic first aid training before graduation. Students often witness injuries at school, work, sports events, and home. Knowing how to respond to bleeding, choking, burns, fainting, and sudden illness can make the minutes before professional help arrives safer and less confusing.
A basic course does not need to turn students into medical experts. It can teach them how to call emergency services clearly, recognize when a situation is dangerous, apply pressure to a wound, and avoid moving someone who may have a serious injury. These are practical skills that many adults wish they had learned earlier. Schools already teach health topics such as nutrition and substance abuse prevention, so first aid would fit naturally into existing health education.
The requirement could also build confidence. In an emergency, people sometimes freeze because they are afraid of doing the wrong thing. Training with simple scenarios can help students remember steps and understand their limits. A one-day or short-unit course would be a reasonable investment in student safety and community preparedness.
Passage B:
Do Not Make First Aid a Graduation Requirement
First aid is valuable, but high schools should not add it as a graduation requirement. Students already face crowded schedules, required testing, career programs, and credit requirements. Adding another required unit may sound simple, but schools must find trained instructors, purchase materials, schedule make-up sessions, and track completion. These costs and responsibilities can be difficult, especially for small districts.
A requirement may also create a false sense of readiness. A student who completes a short course may forget the steps months later or may misunderstand what to do in a serious emergency. Poorly remembered training can be risky. Schools should encourage first aid education, but they should not treat a brief course as proof that every graduate is prepared to respond safely.
There are better options. Schools can offer first aid workshops after school, partner with local emergency medical workers, and provide optional certification opportunities for students who want deeper training. Health classes can include emergency awareness without turning first aid into a graduation requirement. Encouragement and access are better than another rule students must satisfy.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether high schools should require basic first aid training before graduation? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
